# Why Communicate Uncertainty, and to Whom? {#sec-why}

The question of why uncertainty should be communicated at all might seem to
answer itself: honesty demands it, and good decision-making requires it. Yet
in practice, the communication of uncertainty is far from routine. Forecasters
suppress ranges to appear authoritative. Institutions present contested
estimates as settled facts. Scientists avoid probabilistic language for fear
of being misunderstood. Physicians project confidence they do not fully
possess. Understanding why these tendencies are so pervasive — and why
resisting them matters — requires engaging with both the positive case for
uncertainty communication and the documented consequences of its absence.
This section makes that case, then turns to the equally important question
of *to whom* uncertainty should be communicated, and how the needs of
different audiences shape the appropriate approach.

## The Case for Communicating Uncertainty Honestly {#sec-case-for}

The fundamental argument for communicating uncertainty rests on the premise
that "all prediction is inherently uncertain and effective communication of
uncertainty information benefits users' decisions" [@nrc2006]. Because
uncertainty is an inescapable reality of complex systems, no forecast is
complete without a description of its uncertainty [@nrc2006]. To present a
forecast, projection, or scientific estimate without an honest account of its
uncertainty is to misrepresent the state of knowledge — and to deprive
decision-makers of information they need to act wisely.

Honest uncertainty communication serves several distinct and mutually
reinforcing purposes. The first is calibration: if scientists fail to
articulate the boundaries of their knowledge, audiences cannot appropriately
weigh the evidence. As @fischhoff2014 argue, "unless that uncertainty is
communicated effectively, decision makers may put too much or too little faith
in it." Over-reliance on a deterministic forecast that subsequently fails
can be more damaging than the underlying event itself, because it destroys
the institutional credibility that is necessary for future communications to
be trusted. Conversely, communicating genuine uncertainty allows users to
make plans that are robust across a range of possible outcomes, rather than
brittle strategies optimised for a single predicted scenario.

The second purpose is institutional transparency. Qualitative research with
natural hazard scientists reveals that audiences respond positively to honest
acknowledgement of limits: "it always helps if they're open and honest, just
share that they don't quite know" [@doyle2023]. This transparency builds
rather than erodes trust — a finding that directly contradicts the common
assumption among communicators that expressing uncertainty will undermine their
credibility. When institutional actors suppress uncertainty to appear
authoritative, they may achieve short-term confidence at the cost of long-term
trust, particularly when events reveal that the suppressed uncertainty was
real.

The third purpose concerns decision quality. Effective risk management "depends
on information about the full range of possible consequences and associated
probabilities rather than brittle, deterministic planning" [@ipcc2015]. A
decision-maker who knows only the most likely outcome of an intervention is
poorly equipped to plan for the possibility that the outcome falls elsewhere.
A decision-maker who also knows the range of plausible outcomes and their
relative probabilities can build contingency plans, hedge against adverse
possibilities, and calibrate the level of precaution to the actual level of
uncertainty. Uncertainty communication, in this light, is not an admission of
weakness but a prerequisite for sophisticated and adaptive decision-making.

The fourth purpose is scientific progress. @doyle2023 note that openly
admitting data limitations and analytical uncertainties "encourages other
scientists to say 'oh well, let's have a look at it' — and that actually
improves the science." Scientific knowledge advances through the identification
and investigation of gaps; suppressing uncertainty conceals those gaps and
removes the incentive to fill them.

## Consequences of Failing to Communicate Uncertainty {#sec-consequences}

The consequences of failing to communicate uncertainty honestly are well
documented across multiple domains. In medicine, overconfident diagnostic
communication — where physicians present diagnoses with greater certainty than
the evidence warrants — leads to reduced uptake of second opinions, delays in
identifying errors, and unnecessary harm to patients. Studies show that
physicians rated their confidence as only marginally lower for difficult
diagnostic cases than for easy ones, even when their accuracy dropped from
55% to below 6% [@makridakis2016]. A physician who is 64% confident in a
diagnosis that is correct less than 6% of the time is unlikely to seek
confirmatory evidence or disclose uncertainty to the patient — yet both
actions are precisely what would be appropriate.

In finance, the suppression of genuine uncertainty beneath a veneer of
quantified risk contributed directly to the 2007–08 financial crisis.
The belief that complex derivative instruments had been fully de-risked
through mathematical modelling created systemic overconfidence that left
institutions unprepared for the structural failure that followed [@scoones2019].
In climate policy, the failure to communicate the full range of uncertainty
in long-term projections has contributed to both underreaction — when people
treat the lower bound of a range as the likely outcome — and overreaction
— when single extreme scenarios dominate public discourse without appropriate
probabilistic context.

Failing to communicate uncertainty also removes agency from those who would
benefit from it most. @doyle2023 argue that honest communication of
uncertainty "gives the public more agency to make decisions" in relation to
natural hazards and risks. When institutional communicators withhold
uncertainty — even with protective intent — they implicitly decide that the
public cannot handle complex information. The evidence reviewed in this paper
suggests that this assumption is empirically unwarranted: ordinary people
can understand and use probabilistic information, and they make better
decisions when they have it [@raftery2016].

## Audiences and Their Needs {#sec-audiences}

Not all users of uncertain information need the same thing. One of the most
important and consistently supported findings in the uncertainty communication
literature is that different audiences have fundamentally different goals,
different levels of statistical literacy, and different tolerances for
complexity when engaging with uncertain information [@nrc2006]. A format that
serves an expert statistician may actively mislead a general practitioner;
a simplification appropriate for a newspaper headline may be insufficient
for a policy analyst who needs to set regulatory thresholds. Designing
uncertainty communication without first identifying the audience and their
specific decision context is a recipe for poor outcomes regardless of the
technical quality of the underlying analysis.

The most structured audience taxonomy in the reviewed literature is that
developed by @efsa2019, which divides audiences into three tiers based on
their familiarity with scientific assessment, their knowledge of institutional
processes, and the nature of the decision they need to make. This taxonomy
provides a practical organising framework for matching communication methods
to audiences, and its three levels — Entry, Informed, and Technical — are
used throughout @sec-methods to guide the "When to Use" recommendations for
each communication approach.

### Entry Level: General Public, Patients, and General Media {#sec-audience-entry}

The entry-level audience encompasses individuals with "little or no familiarity
with scientific assessment" and "little or no knowledge of institutional
processes and relationships" [@efsa2019]. This group includes members of the
general public engaging with public health communications, patients making
decisions about medical treatments, and consumers of mainstream news media
covering scientific topics. Their engagement with uncertain information is
typically brief, emotionally charged, and embedded in a specific decision
context — whether to evacuate before a storm, whether to accept a medical
intervention, whether to change behaviour in response to a public health
recommendation.

For this audience, the communication goal is often simple behavioural
signalling rather than full probabilistic comprehension. @spiegelhalter2017
recommends providing a "top gist" — a clear, memorable summary of the most
decision-relevant aspect of the uncertain information — before layering any
additional detail. The evidence reviewed by @vanderbles2020 suggests that
communicating uncertainty to this audience using simple numerical ranges
alongside a central estimate is the most robust approach: it does not
substantially decrease trust in the source, and it avoids the ambiguity
problems associated with verbal expressions. Complex distributional displays,
fan charts, and full probability density functions are generally inappropriate
for this audience and should be avoided.

### Informed Level: Policymakers, Non-Governmental Organisations, and Specialist Journalists {#sec-audience-informed}

The informed-level audience has "thorough knowledge of institutional processes
and relationships" and "some or good understanding of scientific assessment
but not advanced" [@efsa2019]. This group includes policymakers and civil
servants who use scientific evidence to inform regulatory decisions, NGO
representatives engaged in evidence-based advocacy, and specialist journalists
covering science, health, or economics beats. They engage with uncertain
information more systematically than entry-level audiences, but they typically
lack the statistical training to interpret complex distributional displays
without guidance.

For this audience, plain-language summaries of uncertainty ranges, clearly
defined categorical labels with explicit quantitative translations, and
narrative accounts of the main sources and implications of uncertainty are
most effective. @efsa2019 recommend providing FAQs that directly address the
most common misunderstandings, and explicitly defining any technical terms
that appear in the communication. The fundamental tension between sender and
receiver preferences documented by @dhami2022 is particularly acute for this
audience: policymakers consistently prefer precise numerical uncertainty
information when making consequential decisions, even though the scientists
who advise them frequently default to vague verbal expressions that offer
face-saving flexibility.

### Technical Level: Domain Experts, Statisticians, and Risk Managers {#sec-audience-technical}

The technical-level audience possesses "advanced understanding of scientific
assessment, use of data, tools and methodologies" [@efsa2019]. This group
includes domain scientists, statisticians, quantitative risk analysts, and
professional risk managers who use uncertainty information as an input to
further modelling, regulatory analysis, or portfolio management. They are
equipped to interpret complex probabilistic displays, to interrogate the
assumptions underlying uncertainty estimates, and to propagate uncertainty
through subsequent analyses.

For this audience, full probability distributions, fan charts, prediction
intervals at multiple levels, and detailed documentation of model assumptions
are not only appropriate but necessary. @raftery2016 identifies a specific
sub-type of technical user — the "decision theorist" — who possesses an
explicit loss function and needs the full predictive distribution to formally
minimise expected loss. This is the only user type for whom the full
distributional complexity of a probabilistic forecast is genuinely required,
and even here, the practical challenges of eliciting and applying a formal
loss function mean that simpler quantile-based summaries often suffice
[@raftery2016].

It is worth noting, as @galvao2019 observe in the context of fan charts for
economic forecasting, that even expert users are not immune to the
deterministic construal error — the tendency to ignore uncertainty bands and
focus on the central estimate. Technical communication should therefore still
include explicit prompts to engage with the full range of uncertainty, not
merely present it and assume it will be used.

@tbl-audiences provides a summary of the three audience tiers, their
characteristic information needs, and the communication formats most
appropriate for each.

| Audience level | Examples | Statistical literacy | Primary need | Recommended formats |
|----------------|----------|---------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Entry** | General public, patients, general media | Low | Simple behavioural signal | Numerical ranges, point estimates, simple icons |
| **Informed** | Policymakers, NGOs, specialist journalists | Moderate | Policy-relevant summary with context | Plain-language summaries, categorical scales with numeric translations, FAQs |
| **Technical** | Domain experts, statisticians, risk managers | High | Full probabilistic detail | Distributions, fan charts, prediction intervals, model documentation |

: Audience tiers and appropriate communication formats, adapted from @efsa2019 {#tbl-audiences}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — EFSA Audience Mapping Grid**

Suggested figure: Figure A.2 in @efsa2019, "Mapping of EFSA's target
audiences for external communications," which plots different societal groups
(e.g., NGOs, general media, risk assessors) on a two-dimensional grid based
on their level of technicality and the nature of their relationship with
institutional communications (short-term vs long-term).

Action needed: Reproduce with permission or recreate as a diagram.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Figure placeholder — Audience Categories and Communication Products**

Suggested figure: The audience categories and communication products table
in @efsa2019, which directly links Entry, Informed, and Technical level
audiences to specific communication products (e.g., plain-language summaries,
scientific publications) and the assumptions made about each group's literacy
and engagement.

Action needed: Reproduce with permission or recreate as a table.
:::

## Disagreements in the Literature {#sec-why-disagreements}

Two significant tensions run through the literature on why and to whom
uncertainty should be communicated, and both have direct implications for
practice.

The first is what might be called the *uncertainty paradox*. On one hand,
robust experimental evidence from @vanderbles2020 demonstrates that the
provision of numerical uncertainty — particularly as a numeric range — does
not substantially decrease public trust in either the numbers or the source.
This is reassuring for communicators who worry that expressing uncertainty
will undermine their credibility. On the other hand, @leffrang2025 find
that highly effective uncertainty visualisations — specifically ensemble
spaghetti plots that accurately depict extreme volatility — can cause
individuals to become "alienated and less likely to take the advice,"
actively reducing their trust in algorithmic guidance. The apparent
contradiction between these findings suggests that the relationship between
uncertainty communication and trust is not linear: modest, well-calibrated
uncertainty communication preserves or even enhances trust, while
overwhelming visualisations of volatility and extreme scenarios may
cross a threshold beyond which the audience disengages. The implication
for practice is that communicators should aim for appropriate honesty about
uncertainty, not maximal transparency at any cost.

The second tension concerns the persistent conflict between sender and
receiver preferences. @dhami2022 document that senders — the scientists,
forecasters, and analysts who generate uncertain information — strongly
prefer to express that uncertainty in vague, verbal terms, because doing
so provides "face-saving" flexibility and reduces their accountability if
a prediction turns out to be wrong. Receivers — the policymakers, public,
and patients who must act on the information — strongly prefer precise,
numerical formats, because they need concrete boundaries to make
consequential decisions. This mismatch is not merely a matter of different
tastes; it has direct consequences for decision quality. When communicators
default to vague verbal expressions for self-protective reasons, they
systematically deprive their audiences of the information those audiences
need most. @dhami2022 conclude that "when it really matters, both senders
and receivers prefer the precise numeric expression of uncertainty" — a
finding that suggests the sender's preference for vagueness may itself
reflect a form of motivated reasoning rather than a genuine assessment
of what serves the audience.

## Good Practice {#sec-why-good-practice}

- **Determine decision relevance first:** Before designing an uncertainty
  communication, identify the specific decision the audience is trying to
  make. @fischhoff2014 recommend assessing whether the audience is "looking
  for a signal, choosing among fixed options, or learning to create options,"
  and tailoring the depth and format of the uncertainty information to that
  specific decision context.
- **Segment the audience:** Never assume a monolithic audience. Practitioners
  must segment audiences into target groups and identify their needs, beliefs,
  and statistical skills before developing communication materials
  [@spiegelhalter2017]. Different groups within the same communication context
  — for example, specialist journalists and general readers of the same news
  outlet — may require fundamentally different treatments of the same
  uncertainty information.
- **Layer the information:** To satisfy multiple audiences simultaneously,
  employ a tiered or layered communication approach. Provide a clear top-line
  gist for entry-level audiences, followed by progressively more complex
  layers — numerical intervals, source explanations, and technical appendices
  — for informed and technical users [@spiegelhalter2017].
- **Develop, test, and evaluate:** Communication products should not be
  released on the assumption that they will be understood as intended.
  @spiegelhalter2017 recommend developing materials with target groups,
  testing them for comprehension, and evaluating their effects on
  decision-making before wide deployment.
- **Resist the face-saving temptation:** Communicators should resist the
  institutional and personal incentives to suppress or vague-ify uncertainty
  for reasons of self-protection. The evidence reviewed in this section
  suggests that honest, calibrated uncertainty communication preserves
  institutional trust over the long run, while suppression of uncertainty
  creates fragility that is revealed when events diverge from deterministic
  predictions.
